title: EGR Position Sensor Circuit High
shortDescription: The EGR position sensor is reporting a voltage higher than the expected range.
fullDescription:
P0406 indicates the Powertrain Control Module (PCM) has detected an abnormally high voltage on the Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) position sensor signal circuit. The sensor monitors the position of the EGR valve to confirm proper operation.

Common causes include a failed EGR position sensor, corroded or open signal wiring, or a shorted 5V reference line. Extended driving with this fault can increase NOx emissions and may contribute to detonation under load.
safetyImpact: Increased NOx emissions and possible engine pinging. Vehicle remains drivable but will fail emissions testing.

causes.cause: Open or shorted wiring to EGR sensor

fixSteps.title: Scan and verify code
fixSteps.instruction: Connect OBD-II scanner, record freeze frame data, clear code and test drive to confirm it returns.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Visually inspect EGR sensor connector and harness for damage, corrosion, or disconnection.

fixSteps.title: Test sensor voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Backprobe the sensor signal wire with key on. Voltage should fall within manufacturer specification (typically 0.5-4.5V).

fixSteps.title: Replace EGR valve/sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If sensor is integrated, replace the EGR valve assembly. Clear codes and retest.

faq.a: Short distances yes, but have it repaired soon to avoid emissions failure and potential engine damage.

faq.q: Will cleaning the EGR valve fix this?
faq.a: Sometimes — carbon can bind the valve and affect sensor readings. Cleaning is a low-cost first step.

title: Secondary Air Injection Switching Valve A Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: Fault detected in the secondary air injection switching valve control circuit.
fullDescription:
P0412 sets when the PCM detects an electrical fault in the secondary air injection switching valve A circuit. The secondary air injection system pumps fresh air into the exhaust during cold starts to help the catalyst reach operating temperature faster.

Typical causes include a failed switching valve, corroded connectors exposed to exhaust heat, or internal PCM driver failure. This system primarily affects cold-start emissions.
safetyImpact: Increased cold-start emissions only. Vehicle drives normally once warmed up.

fixSteps.title: Verify fault
fixSteps.instruction: Scan code, record freeze frame and review history.

fixSteps.title: Inspect valve and wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Locate switching valve, inspect connector for corrosion and test continuity.

fixSteps.title: Replace valve
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the switching valve solenoid if defective. Clear code and complete drive cycle.

faq.a: No, it affects only cold-start emissions. Repair at your convenience, but it will cause emissions test failure.

title: Main Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The main catalytic converter on bank 1 is no longer operating at required efficiency.
fullDescription:
P0422 indicates the PCM has compared pre- and post-catalyst oxygen sensor signals and concluded that the main catalytic converter on bank 1 is no longer storing and releasing oxygen efficiently. This is similar to P0420 but specifies the main catalyst rather than a generic efficiency failure.

Typical causes include a worn catalyst (age/mileage), contamination from oil or coolant burning, or faulty downstream oxygen sensors providing misleading data.
safetyImpact: Increased emissions. Vehicle drives normally but will fail emissions testing.

causes.cause: Engine running rich from other faults

fixSteps.title: Verify and diagnose
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for related codes first. Misfires or O2 sensor codes must be fixed first.

fixSteps.title: Monitor O2 sensor data
fixSteps.instruction: View live data from upstream and downstream O2 sensors. Downstream should be steady compared to switching upstream.

fixSteps.title: Inspect exhaust
fixSteps.instruction: Check for leaks, physical damage or rattling in the catalytic converter.

fixSteps.title: Replace catalyst
fixSteps.instruction: If diagnostics confirm catalyst failure, replace converter with an OE-equivalent unit.

faq.q: How long can I drive with P0422?
faq.a: Vehicle is drivable but emissions will be elevated. Address within weeks to avoid emissions fines.

title: Main Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
shortDescription: Bank 2 main catalytic converter is operating below required efficiency.
fullDescription:
P0432 is the bank 2 equivalent of P0422. The PCM has determined that the main catalyst on bank 2 (the side opposite the #1 cylinder) is no longer converting exhaust pollutants efficiently.

This code is common on V6/V8 engines. It often appears alongside P0422 when both catalysts age together, but can appear alone if only one bank is affected.
safetyImpact: Emissions-related. Vehicle drives normally.

fixSteps.title: Scan for companion codes
fixSteps.instruction: Address any misfire, fuel trim, or O2 sensor codes first.

fixSteps.title: Compare O2 sensor data
fixSteps.instruction: View bank 2 pre/post O2 sensors in live data to confirm catalyst efficiency fault.

fixSteps.title: Replace bank 2 catalyst
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the bank 2 catalytic converter with OE-spec part.

faq.a: If only bank 2 fails it is acceptable to replace just that side, but if the vehicle is high-mileage the other bank may fail soon.

title: Evaporative Emission Control Purge Valve Circuit Open
shortDescription: The EVAP purge valve control circuit is open.
fullDescription:
P0444 is set when the PCM cannot complete the circuit driving the evaporative emissions purge valve. The purge valve meters stored fuel vapors from the charcoal canister into the intake manifold.

An open circuit means either the valve coil has failed, the wiring is damaged, or the PCM driver has failed.
safetyImpact: Minor fuel smell, emissions fault. No drivability concern.

fixSteps.title: Check purge valve connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the valve (usually on intake) and inspect its connector.

fixSteps.title: Test valve resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure coil resistance — typically 20-30 ohms. Open reading means bad valve.

fixSteps.title: Replace purge valve
fixSteps.instruction: Install new valve and clear the code.

faq.q: Is P0444 safe to drive with?
faq.a: Yes, it is an emissions-only fault that does not affect drivability.

title: Evaporative Emission Leak Detected (Loose Fuel Cap)
shortDescription: The EVAP system has a large leak, most commonly a loose or missing fuel cap.
fullDescription:
P0457 indicates the PCM has detected a large evaporative system leak. Unlike the smaller P0455/P0456, this code often points directly at a loose, missing, or failed fuel cap.

After tightening or replacing the cap, the code may take several drive cycles to clear automatically, or you can clear it with a scan tool.
safetyImpact: Fuel vapor escape into atmosphere. No drivability effect.

fixSteps.title: Check fuel cap
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and reinstall fuel cap until it clicks 3 times.

fixSteps.title: Inspect cap seal
fixSteps.instruction: Look for cracks, debris or deformation in the rubber seal.

fixSteps.title: Replace cap if needed
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new OE-spec fuel cap and clear the code.

faq.q: How long until the light turns off?
faq.a: After tightening the cap, the light typically clears within 2-3 drive cycles (50-100 miles).

title: Evaporative Emission System High Purge Flow
shortDescription: The EVAP system is flowing more purge vapor than expected.
fullDescription:
P0496 sets when the PCM detects excessive vacuum in the fuel tank or EVAP system, suggesting the purge valve is flowing when it should be closed or flowing more than commanded.

Common causes include a stuck-open purge valve, a vacuum leak in the EVAP lines allowing unmetered flow, or a failed vent valve creating incorrect system pressure readings.
safetyImpact: Minor drivability issues possible (slight rough idle). Emissions-related.

fixSteps.title: Scan and freeze frame
fixSteps.instruction: Record conditions when the code set.

fixSteps.title: Test purge valve
fixSteps.instruction: With engine off, apply vacuum to the valve — it should hold. If it leaks, replace.

fixSteps.title: Inspect EVAP hoses
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect all EVAP hoses from canister to engine for cracks or disconnections.

faq.a: A severely stuck-open purge valve can cause rough idle or occasional stalling at stops.

title: Brake Switch A/B Correlation
shortDescription: The two brake switch signals do not agree with each other.
fullDescription:
Most modern vehicles use two redundant brake pedal switches — one that signals 'pressed', one that signals 'released'. P0504 is set when these two signals contradict each other, suggesting one switch is stuck or misaligned.

This can affect cruise control, push-button start, and transmission shift interlock. It is commonly triggered by a failed or misadjusted brake pedal switch assembly.
safetyImpact: Cruise control may be disabled, shift interlock may not release. Taillights may stay on or fail to illuminate.
symptoms: Brake lights stay on or fail

fixSteps.title: Inspect brake light operation
fixSteps.instruction: Have helper press brake; confirm brake lights turn on and off correctly.

fixSteps.title: Adjust or replace switch
fixSteps.instruction: Remove switch from bracket, check plunger engagement, replace if damaged.

fixSteps.title: Clear code and test
fixSteps.instruction: Clear code, test cruise control and shift release.

faq.a: Short distances, yes — but shift interlock failure can strand you at a stop sign. Fix promptly.

title: Idle Air Control System Circuit Low
shortDescription: The idle air control circuit voltage is lower than expected.
fullDescription:
P0508 indicates the PCM has detected the idle air control (IAC) circuit voltage is below the normal operating range. On older vehicles this refers to the IAC motor or solenoid; on newer vehicles it may relate to an electronic throttle body.

Causes include a shorted IAC valve, wiring shorted to ground, or a failed PCM driver.
safetyImpact: Rough or unstable idle. Possible stalling at stops.

fixSteps.title: Test IAC valve
fixSteps.instruction: Unplug IAC and measure resistance per specification. Out-of-spec = replace.

fixSteps.title: Check wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check each IAC control wire for short to ground with valve disconnected.

fixSteps.title: Replace IAC valve
fixSteps.instruction: Install new IAC valve and clear code.

faq.q: Can I clean the IAC valve instead of replacing?
faq.a: Yes, carbon buildup can cause issues. Throttle body cleaner applied with IAC removed often restores function.

title: Idle Air Control Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: General malfunction in the idle air control circuit.
fullDescription:
P0511 is a generic idle air control circuit malfunction. The PCM detected an unspecified electrical problem in the IAC control or feedback circuit.

Causes range from wiring issues to valve failure. Diagnosis begins with the IAC valve itself and its wiring harness.
safetyImpact: Rough or erratic idle, stalling possible at low speeds.

fixSteps.title: Clean throttle body and IAC
fixSteps.instruction: Remove IAC and throttle body, spray with throttle body cleaner.

fixSteps.title: Test for vacuum leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect intake boot, gaskets and hoses for leaks.

fixSteps.title: Replace IAC if needed
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning doesn't resolve the code, replace IAC valve.

faq.a: Often yes. Carbon fouling of the IAC is one of the most common causes.

title: Incorrect Immobilizer Key
shortDescription: The PCM does not recognize the key being used.
fullDescription:
P0513 sets when the immobilizer system does not recognize the transponder in the ignition key. The vehicle may crank but not start, or may run briefly and shut down.

This is common after using a non-programmed key, a dead key battery (for proximity systems), or a damaged antenna ring around the ignition.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not start or may shut down while driving.

fixSteps.title: Try the backup key
fixSteps.instruction: Use a different working key to confirm the issue is key-specific.

fixSteps.title: Check battery in fob
fixSteps.instruction: Replace key fob battery on proximity systems.

fixSteps.title: Have key programmed
fixSteps.instruction: Visit dealer or automotive locksmith to program key to immobilizer.

faq.q: Why does the car start and then die?
faq.a: The immobilizer shuts off fuel injection after a few seconds when it doesn't recognize the key.

title: System Voltage Malfunction
shortDescription: The PCM has detected an abnormal system voltage.
fullDescription:
P0560 is a general voltage fault set when the PCM detects battery voltage outside expected range during operation. This typically points to charging system problems — a failing alternator, bad battery, or poor cable connections.

Unlike P0562 (low) or P0563 (high), P0560 is usually an intermittent voltage issue or noise on the voltage sensing circuit.
safetyImpact: Electrical accessories may behave erratically. Vehicle may stall if voltage drops too low.

fixSteps.title: Test battery
fixSteps.instruction: Load test battery to confirm it holds charge.

fixSteps.title: Test alternator
fixSteps.instruction: With engine running, voltage at battery should be 13.8-14.7V.

fixSteps.title: Clean connections
fixSteps.instruction: Clean battery terminals and alternator grounds.

fixSteps.title: Replace faulty component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace alternator or battery based on test results.

faq.q: Could a jump-starter have caused P0560?
faq.a: Yes, voltage spikes from jump-starting or bad jumper procedures can set this code. Clear and retest.

title: Cruise Control On Signal Malfunction
shortDescription: The cruise control ON signal is not behaving as expected.
fullDescription:
P0565 is set when the PCM detects the cruise control ON/OFF switch input is stuck, missing, or toggling unexpectedly. The fault is in the switch circuit, not the cruise control actuator.

Common causes include a failed cruise control switch on the steering wheel, damaged clock-spring under the airbag, or wiring issues.
safetyImpact: Cruise control may be disabled or act erratically.

fixSteps.title: Test switch operation
fixSteps.instruction: Using scan tool live data, watch switch state while pressing buttons.

fixSteps.title: Check steering wheel contacts
fixSteps.instruction: If switch appears intact, suspect clock-spring after airbag removal.

fixSteps.title: Replace faulty component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace switch or clock-spring as appropriate.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive without cruise control?
faq.a: Yes, P0565 does not affect normal vehicle operation.

title: Cruise Control/Brake Switch A Circuit Low
shortDescription: Brake switch A circuit voltage is low.
fullDescription:
P0572 is set when the PCM sees the brake switch A signal stuck low — the PCM thinks the brake is always pressed. This disables cruise control and may affect shift interlock.

Common causes include a failed brake switch, wiring short to ground, or a corroded connector at the switch.
safetyImpact: Cruise disabled. Brake lights may stay on draining battery.

fixSteps.title: Inspect brake lights
fixSteps.instruction: Check if brake lights are on with pedal released — confirms stuck switch.

fixSteps.title: Adjust or replace switch
fixSteps.instruction: Remove brake switch from bracket and replace.

faq.a: Yes — if brake lights are stuck on, battery can drain overnight.

title: Control Module VSS Output A Malfunction
shortDescription: The PCM's vehicle speed signal output A is malfunctioning.
fullDescription:
P0608 indicates the PCM's internal vehicle speed signal output driver has detected a fault. This output feeds vehicle speed information to other modules like the instrument cluster, transmission, or ABS.

Causes include shorts in the shared VSS bus, PCM internal failure, or a faulty receiving module drawing incorrect current.
safetyImpact: Speedometer, odometer or transmission may behave incorrectly.

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Pull codes from all modules, not just PCM.

fixSteps.title: Isolate bus fault
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect receiving modules one at a time to identify a shorted load.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace
fixSteps.instruction: Repair wiring, replace faulty module, or reprogram PCM as needed.

faq.q: Is this always a bad PCM?
faq.a: No — most often it is wiring or a downstream module, not the PCM itself.

title: Control Module Vehicle Options Error
shortDescription: PCM detected a vehicle options configuration mismatch.
fullDescription:
P0610 indicates the PCM's stored vehicle options data does not match what is physically present on the vehicle. This often appears after PCM replacement/reflash when the PCM has not been properly configured for the specific vehicle.

It can also set from corruption during a failed reflash or voltage drop during programming.
safetyImpact: Various systems may not function or function incorrectly.

fixSteps.title: Check PCM programming
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm PCM VIN matches vehicle and options are set correctly.

fixSteps.title: Reflash PCM
fixSteps.instruction: Reflash with correct vehicle calibration.

faq.q: Can I fix P0610 without a dealer?
faq.a: In most cases no — OEM-level tools are needed to reprogram options.

title: Power Steering Control Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: Power steering control circuit fault detected.
fullDescription:
P0635 sets when the PCM detects a problem in the electric power steering (EPS) control circuit. On modern vehicles this controls the EPS motor or the variable-assist solenoid on hydraulic systems.

This can cause loss of power steering assist or erratic assist behavior, making the vehicle harder to steer.
safetyImpact: Loss of power steering assist — vehicle becomes hard to steer, especially at low speeds.

fixSteps.title: Check battery voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm battery is healthy — low voltage often triggers EPS faults.

fixSteps.title: Scan EPS module
fixSteps.instruction: Full-system scan usually reveals specific EPS subfault.

fixSteps.title: Inspect EPS wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect harness to steering rack/column, which often chafes against body panels.

fixSteps.title: Replace failed component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace EPS motor or module based on diagnosis.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive with no power steering?
faq.a: The vehicle remains controllable but steering effort is much higher. Avoid highway driving and repair promptly.

title: Sensor Reference Voltage A Circuit Open
shortDescription: The 5V reference circuit A from the PCM is open.
fullDescription:
P0641 is set when the PCM cannot maintain its 5V reference voltage on circuit A. This 5V line powers many sensors (TPS, MAP, CKP, CMP, etc.), and when it fails many sensors report incorrectly at the same time.

Often caused by a shorted sensor pulling the reference line down, damaged wiring, or an internal PCM regulator failure.
safetyImpact: Multiple sensor failures, engine may run poorly or not at all.

fixSteps.title: Scan for all codes
fixSteps.instruction: A full scan often lists many sensor faults sharing the 5V reference.

fixSteps.title: Measure 5V reference
fixSteps.instruction: Backprobe each sensor's 5V wire with KOEO. Should read ~5.0V.

fixSteps.title: Isolate shorted sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect sensors one at a time; when 5V returns, the last disconnected is the culprit.

fixSteps.title: Repair wire or replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Fix short or replace failed sensor.

faq.q: Is the PCM bad when P0641 sets?
faq.a: Usually no — a shorted sensor or chafed wire is much more common than a failed PCM regulator.

title: Glow Plug Module Control Circuit
shortDescription: Fault detected in the glow plug module control circuit.
fullDescription:
P0670 sets on diesel engines when the PCM detects a fault in the command or feedback circuit of the glow plug control module. Glow plugs are essential for cold starting and to control emissions at low temperatures.

Causes include a failed glow plug module, wiring issues between PCM and module, or one or more failed glow plugs pulling excessive current.
safetyImpact: Hard cold starts, excessive cold-start smoke.

fixSteps.title: Test individual glow plugs
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance — typically 0.5-2 ohms. Open = dead plug.

fixSteps.title: Inspect GP module
fixSteps.instruction: Check module connectors for corrosion; scan for subfault codes.

fixSteps.title: Replace defective parts
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed glow plugs and/or control module.

faq.q: Can I drive the car with a bad glow plug?
faq.a: Yes, in warm weather, but cold starts will be rough. Not recommended for winter driving.

title: Transmission Control System Range/Performance
shortDescription: The transmission control system is operating outside the expected range.
fullDescription:
P0701 is a general transmission performance fault, often set when the transmission control module (TCM) detects that shift timing, pressure, or gear selection is not behaving as expected but no more specific code has been stored.

Causes can include internal transmission issues, low fluid, failed solenoids, or a failing TCM. This code usually accompanies additional specific codes.
safetyImpact: Transmission may enter limp mode. Shift quality will be poor.

fixSteps.title: Check fluid level and condition
fixSteps.instruction: Check transmission fluid — should be red and clear, not brown or burnt.

fixSteps.title: Scan for related codes
fixSteps.instruction: P0701 usually comes with a specific solenoid or ratio code — address those first.

fixSteps.title: Service transmission
fixSteps.instruction: Drain/fill with correct ATF, replace filter if possible.

fixSteps.title: Deeper repair
fixSteps.instruction: If above does not resolve, transmission service by specialist needed.

faq.q: Does P0701 mean my transmission is failing?
faq.a: Not necessarily — it often starts with fluid or solenoid issues, which are much less costly than a rebuild.

title: Transmission Range Sensor Circuit Low
shortDescription: Range sensor signal voltage is below the expected range.
fullDescription:
P0707 sets when the TCM/PCM detects the transmission range (gear selector position) sensor voltage is stuck low. The range sensor tells the TCM which gear (P/R/N/D/2/1) the driver has selected.

Causes include a failed or misaligned range sensor, wiring shorted to ground, or internal transmission shift linkage problems.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not start, may start in wrong gear, or transmission may default to limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Check shifter alignment
fixSteps.instruction: Verify shifter moves crisply through all positions.

fixSteps.title: Test range sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Use scan tool live data to confirm sensor signal matches selected gear.

fixSteps.title: Replace range sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace sensor, adjust per service manual.

faq.a: Neutral safety function uses the range sensor. If PCM can't confirm P or N, starter is disabled.

title: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: Transmission fluid temperature signal is not changing as expected.
fullDescription:
P0711 sets when the TCM detects the transmission fluid temperature (TFT) sensor signal does not rise as the transmission warms up, or the reading is stuck at an unrealistic value.

Common causes include a failed TFT sensor (often integrated into the internal wiring harness), wiring issues inside the transmission pan, or low fluid level.
safetyImpact: Delayed torque converter lockup, poor shift quality until TCM trusts temperature data.

fixSteps.title: Check fluid level
fixSteps.instruction: Check ATF level and condition first.

fixSteps.title: Live data check
fixSteps.instruction: Scan ATF temp from cold start — should rise steadily with driving.

fixSteps.title: Replace internal harness/sensor
fixSteps.instruction: On most units this means dropping the pan and replacing TFT or harness.

faq.q: Can I keep driving with P0711?
faq.a: Short term yes, but shifting quality will suffer and fuel economy will drop.

title: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low
shortDescription: Transmission fluid temperature sensor voltage is too low.
fullDescription:
P0712 sets when the TFT sensor signal indicates impossibly high transmission fluid temperature (low sensor voltage = high temperature on most designs). The TCM uses fluid temperature to modify shift timing and pressure, so a bad reading affects shift quality.

Common causes include a shorted TFT sensor, pinched internal wiring, or a shorted external harness between the transmission and TCM.
safetyImpact: TCM may lock out torque converter, force harsh shifts, or enter limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Measure sensor signal
fixSteps.instruction: With engine cold, sensor voltage should be near reference. If near 0V, sensor or wiring is shorted.

fixSteps.title: Inspect harness
fixSteps.instruction: Drop pan, inspect internal wiring for pinching or melting.

fixSteps.title: Replace shorted component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace sensor or harness and refill with correct ATF.

faq.a: Usually no — P0712 is typically a sensor fault reporting false high temperature.

title: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor A Circuit High
shortDescription: Transmission fluid temperature sensor voltage is too high.
fullDescription:
P0713 indicates the TFT sensor signal is pegged high, suggesting the TCM reads an impossibly low fluid temperature (e.g., below -40°F). The circuit is likely open.

This is the opposite of P0712 and points to an open sensor or broken wiring.
safetyImpact: TCM may delay torque converter lockup indefinitely, hurting fuel economy.

fixSteps.title: Check transmission connector
fixSteps.instruction: Unplug external transmission connector, check for corrosion/damage.

fixSteps.title: Measure sensor resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Backprobe or use breakout — compare to specification at known temperature.

fixSteps.title: Replace TFT/harness
fixSteps.instruction: Replace internal TFT or harness, refill ATF.

faq.a: Long-term yes — without torque converter lockup, fluid gets hotter and wears faster.

title: Output Speed Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: Transmission output speed sensor signal is erratic.
fullDescription:
P0721 sets when the TCM detects that the output speed sensor (OSS) signal is present but noisy, intermittent, or implausible. The OSS measures transmission output shaft rotation and is critical for shift timing.

Causes include a worn speed sensor, metal debris on the sensor magnet, damaged tone ring/reluctor, or loose sensor mounting.
safetyImpact: Harsh shifting, speedometer fluctuations, possible limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Remove and inspect sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Unbolt OSS, look for metal fuzz on magnetic tip.

fixSteps.title: Clean or replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Clean tip or install new OSS.

fixSteps.title: If persistent, inspect tone ring
fixSteps.instruction: Damaged reluctor requires transmission disassembly.

faq.a: If erratic, don't rely on it. Replace the sensor promptly.

title: Output Speed Sensor No Signal
shortDescription: No signal detected from the transmission output speed sensor.
fullDescription:
P0722 sets when the TCM receives no signal from the output speed sensor while the vehicle is moving. The speedometer may read zero, and the TCM cannot properly schedule shifts.

Causes include a failed OSS, open wiring, shattered tone ring, or sensor pushed out from the bore.
safetyImpact: Speedometer at zero, severe shifting issues, potential limp mode.

causes.cause: Open wiring from OSS to TCM

fixSteps.title: Check connector
fixSteps.instruction: Verify OSS connector is fully seated and not corroded.

fixSteps.title: Test sensor continuity
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance of OSS coil — open = bad sensor.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new OSS and clear code.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive with P0722?
faq.a: No — speedometer is inoperative and shift behavior is unpredictable. Tow or drive short distance only.

title: Gear 6 Incorrect Ratio
shortDescription: The transmission is not achieving the correct ratio in 6th gear.
fullDescription:
P0729 sets when the TCM detects the input-to-output speed ratio does not match the expected 6th gear ratio. This indicates slippage, a stuck shift solenoid, or mechanical problems inside the transmission.

Most commonly caused by worn clutches or a failed solenoid preventing proper engagement.
safetyImpact: Slipping in 6th gear, possible limp mode, reduced highway fuel economy.

fixSteps.title: Check ATF
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect for dark color, burnt smell.

fixSteps.title: Diagnose solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Have shop test solenoid function with advanced scan tool.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild/replace
fixSteps.instruction: Internal damage generally requires transmission service.

faq.q: Can a fluid change fix P0729?
faq.a: Occasionally, if debris is affecting solenoid operation. Usually points to internal wear.

title: Gear 5 Incorrect Ratio
shortDescription: Transmission gear ratio in 5th is incorrect.
fullDescription:
P0735 indicates the TCM detected a ratio mismatch in 5th gear. The input shaft and output shaft speeds do not correspond to the expected 5th gear mechanical ratio.

This usually points to clutch slippage, a failed solenoid, or internal hydraulic problems.
safetyImpact: Slipping at highway speeds. Overheat risk for transmission.

fixSteps.title: Check ATF level and condition
fixSteps.instruction: Top off or change if needed.

fixSteps.title: Scan for solenoid codes
fixSteps.instruction: Look for companion P07xx codes.

fixSteps.title: Transmission service
fixSteps.instruction: Valve body or rebuild by a specialist.

faq.a: Not always — start with fluid, scan, and a trusted transmission shop diagnosis.

title: Reverse Incorrect Ratio
shortDescription: Transmission reverse gear ratio is incorrect.
fullDescription:
P0736 is set when the TCM detects a ratio error while in reverse. The reverse clutch or band is likely slipping, or a solenoid is not energizing correctly.

Reverse-specific failures are frequent in older high-mileage transmissions.
safetyImpact: Reverse may slip or not engage. Forward gears may still work.

fixSteps.title: Check ATF
fixSteps.instruction: Low fluid often causes reverse failure first.

fixSteps.title: Scan codes
fixSteps.instruction: Check companion codes for solenoid or pressure faults.

fixSteps.title: Transmission repair
fixSteps.instruction: Shop diagnosis, likely rebuild.

faq.a: In theory yes, but parking becomes very difficult. Fix promptly.

title: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Stuck On
shortDescription: Torque converter clutch is stuck in the applied position.
fullDescription:
P0742 sets when the TCM detects the torque converter clutch (TCC) is applied when it should be released, or stays on when commanded off. This is usually caused by a stuck TCC solenoid, debris in the valve body, or a stuck lockup valve.

Result is the engine stalls when coming to a stop with transmission in gear, because the engine and transmission cannot decouple.
safetyImpact: Engine stalls when stopping. Major safety concern in traffic.

fixSteps.title: Flush fluid and replace filter
fixSteps.instruction: Debris may be preventing solenoid return. Fresh ATF and filter can help.

fixSteps.title: Replace TCC solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Drop valve body, replace TCC solenoid.

fixSteps.title: Inspect valve body
fixSteps.instruction: If problem persists, valve body requires service.

faq.q: Is it dangerous to drive with P0742?
faq.a: Yes — the engine can stall when coming to stops, leaving you without power steering or brake assist briefly.

title: Pressure Control Solenoid A Performance/Stuck Off
shortDescription: Pressure control solenoid A is stuck off or not performing.
fullDescription:
P0746 sets when the TCM commands the pressure control solenoid A (PCS-A) and does not see the expected change in transmission line pressure. The solenoid is likely stuck in the off position, not moving, or mechanically seized.

PCS solenoids regulate transmission line pressure — if stuck, shifts may be extremely harsh or slipping may occur.
safetyImpact: Harsh shifts, slipping, possible limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Check ATF
fixSteps.instruction: Fresh fluid and filter may resolve debris-related solenoid issues.

fixSteps.title: Test solenoid electrically
fixSteps.instruction: Check resistance and wiring.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid/valve body
fixSteps.instruction: Service valve body or replace pressure solenoid.

faq.q: Will a fluid change fix this?
faq.a: Sometimes — debris is a common cause. Always try fresh ATF and filter first.

title: Pressure Control Solenoid A Stuck On
shortDescription: Pressure control solenoid A is stuck on.
fullDescription:
P0747 sets when the TCM commands the pressure control solenoid A to reduce line pressure and the pressure does not drop. The solenoid is mechanically stuck on, or the valve body passage is blocked.

The transmission may shift very harshly since line pressure stays maxed regardless of load.
safetyImpact: Very harsh shifting, potential transmission damage with prolonged driving.

fixSteps.title: Fluid and filter service
fixSteps.instruction: Drain, replace filter, refill with correct ATF.

fixSteps.title: Valve body service
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean/replace valve body and solenoid.

faq.q: Why does my car shift so hard now?
faq.a: Stuck-on pressure control solenoid applies full line pressure on every shift. Repair promptly.

title: Shift Solenoid A Stuck On
shortDescription: Shift solenoid A is stuck on.
fullDescription:
P0752 sets when the TCM commands shift solenoid A (SSA) off and detects it is still on. This can lock the transmission in one gear or cause no-shift conditions.

Causes include a mechanically stuck solenoid, debris blocking the return, or an electrical short.
safetyImpact: Transmission may not shift out of a single gear. Reduced drivability.

fixSteps.title: Fluid service
fixSteps.instruction: Drain and refill, replace filter.

fixSteps.title: Solenoid replacement
fixSteps.instruction: Replace SSA solenoid in valve body.

faq.q: Can I drive stuck in one gear?
faq.a: Briefly, to a shop. Avoid highway speeds if stuck in low gear.

title: Shift Solenoid A Electrical
shortDescription: Electrical fault in shift solenoid A circuit.
fullDescription:
P0753 indicates an electrical fault (open or short) in the shift solenoid A circuit. The TCM cannot properly control SSA.

Causes include a failed solenoid coil, broken internal transmission harness, or external wiring issue between TCM and transmission connector.
safetyImpact: Harsh or no shifts, likely limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Test solenoid resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure at external connector; compare to spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Replace SSA inside the transmission pan.

faq.a: Most commonly yes, but always check wiring and connector first — cheaper fix if that is the issue.

title: Shift Solenoid B Stuck On
shortDescription: Shift solenoid B is stuck in the on position.
fullDescription:
P0757 sets when the TCM commands shift solenoid B off but detects it is still hydraulically on. Common on 4-speed automatics where solenoid B controls engagement of specific gears.

Causes include debris in the valve body, a stuck solenoid plunger, or an electrical issue energizing it constantly.
safetyImpact: Limited gear selection, harsh or missing shifts.

fixSteps.title: Fluid and filter change
fixSteps.instruction: Drain, refill, replace filter.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Replace SSB solenoid in valve body.

faq.a: Yes — they are wear items and high-mileage transmissions often need solenoid service.

title: Shift Solenoid B Electrical
shortDescription: Electrical fault in shift solenoid B circuit.
fullDescription:
P0758 indicates an electrical fault (open, short to ground, or short to power) in the SSB control circuit. The TCM cannot reliably command solenoid B.

Diagnosis starts with harness and solenoid resistance checks.
safetyImpact: Limp mode, harsh or no shifts.

fixSteps.title: Measure solenoid resistance
fixSteps.instruction: From TCM connector or transmission connector — spec varies.

fixSteps.title: Inspect harness
fixSteps.instruction: Check external transmission connector for corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid/harness
fixSteps.instruction: Replace SSB or internal harness.

faq.q: Will it damage the transmission to drive with this?
faq.a: Short trips to a shop are OK. Extended driving in limp mode should be avoided.

title: Shift Solenoid C Stuck On
shortDescription: Shift solenoid C is stuck on.
fullDescription:
P0762 is set when the TCM commands SSC off but detects it remains active. Shift solenoid C typically controls engagement of higher gears or overdrive.

Mechanical sticking, debris, or electrical shorts are common causes.
safetyImpact: Stuck in a gear, missing upshifts/downshifts.

fixSteps.title: Fluid service
fixSteps.instruction: Replace ATF and filter first.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Replace SSC in valve body.

faq.a: Yes, clutch material and metal fines accumulate over time and can jam solenoid plungers.

title: Shift Solenoid C Electrical
shortDescription: Electrical fault in shift solenoid C circuit.
fullDescription:
P0763 indicates an electrical fault in the SSC circuit. Similar to P0753/P0758 but for solenoid C. The TCM cannot reliably control SSC.

Check external harness and connector first — these are frequently damaged at the transmission bulkhead.
safetyImpact: Shift issues, limp mode likely.

fixSteps.title: Test resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Compare to manufacturer specification.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid or harness
fixSteps.instruction: Install new SSC or internal harness kit.

faq.a: No — always check the external transmission connector first for corrosion.

title: Shift Solenoid D Performance
shortDescription: Shift solenoid D is not performing correctly.
fullDescription:
P0766 is set when the TCM commands SSD and does not see the expected hydraulic/mechanical response. This usually points to internal sticking, debris, or a weak solenoid return spring.

On some transmissions SSD is the 4-5 shift or overdrive solenoid.
safetyImpact: Shift timing off, slipping possible, fuel economy loss.

fixSteps.title: Check ATF
fixSteps.instruction: Service fluid first.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Swap SSD in the valve body.

faq.q: Can I just live with this?
faq.a: Not recommended — slipping generates heat, which accelerates transmission wear.

title: Pressure Control Solenoid B Performance/Stuck Off
shortDescription: Pressure control solenoid B is stuck off or performing poorly.
fullDescription:
P0776 sets when the PCS-B is not changing pressure as commanded. This solenoid controls a different clutch circuit than PCS-A. Symptoms often include specific slipping or harsh engagement of one clutch pack.

Fluid service often helps if debris is causing the sticking.
safetyImpact: Slipping or harsh engagement in affected gear.

fixSteps.title: ATF service
fixSteps.instruction: Drain, refill, filter.

fixSteps.title: Valve body service
fixSteps.instruction: Replace solenoid or valve body assembly.

faq.a: Modern transmissions use multiple PCS solenoids to regulate different clutch/band pressures independently.

title: Pressure Control Solenoid B Stuck On
shortDescription: Pressure control solenoid B is stuck on.
fullDescription:
P0777 indicates PCS-B is stuck in the on (maximum pressure) position. This causes harsh engagement and can accelerate wear in the affected clutch pack.

Causes are similar to P0776 — contamination, mechanical sticking, or electrical faults.
safetyImpact: Very harsh shifts, possible premature clutch wear.

fixSteps.title: Fluid service
fixSteps.instruction: Drain and refill, replace filter.

fixSteps.title: Valve body service
fixSteps.instruction: Replace solenoid or valve body.

faq.q: Will driving with this damage my transmission?
faq.a: Yes — harsh engagement accelerates clutch wear. Repair promptly.

title: Pressure Control Solenoid B Electrical
shortDescription: Electrical fault in PCS-B circuit.
fullDescription:
P0778 sets on detection of an open, short-to-ground, or short-to-power on the PCS-B circuit. The TCM can no longer control pressure for that clutch circuit.

Check external wiring and transmission connector first.
safetyImpact: Limp mode. Harsh or non-existent shifts.

fixSteps.title: Measure resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Compare to spec at transmission connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Replace PCS-B.

faq.q: Is this always an internal problem?
faq.a: Not always. Check the external connector and harness before dropping the pan.

title: Shift Malfunction
shortDescription: A general transmission shift malfunction has been detected.
fullDescription:
P0780 is a generic shift malfunction that does not specify which shift. The TCM has detected abnormal shift behavior — wrong gear, slipping during shift, wrong timing, etc.

Usually accompanied by more specific codes. Start by reading those first and address them in order.
safetyImpact: Poor shift quality, limp mode possible.

fixSteps.title: Scan all codes
fixSteps.instruction: Address companion codes first.

fixSteps.title: ATF service
fixSteps.instruction: Fresh fluid and filter.

fixSteps.title: Professional diagnosis
fixSteps.instruction: If codes persist, visit transmission specialist.

faq.a: Not always. Fluid and solenoid issues are common and much cheaper than a rebuild.

title: 1-2 Shift Malfunction
shortDescription: The 1-2 upshift is not occurring correctly.
fullDescription:
P0781 specifies the 1-2 shift is slipping, delayed, or not completing. This is often caused by problems with the 1-2 shift solenoid, fluid pressure to the 2nd gear clutch/band, or internal clutch wear.

Common in high-mileage automatics.
safetyImpact: Harsh or missed 1-2 shift. Possible flare.

fixSteps.title: Check ATF
fixSteps.instruction: Top off or service.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Replace 1-2 shift solenoid.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild if needed
fixSteps.instruction: If clutch pack is worn, rebuild required.

faq.a: 1-2 shift handles the highest torque multiplication and wears quickly when fluid is old.

title: 2-3 Shift Malfunction
shortDescription: The 2-3 upshift is not completing correctly.
fullDescription:
P0782 indicates the 2-3 shift is slipping, harsh, or delayed. Similar causes to P0781 but for the 2-3 shift: solenoid, clutch, or valve body issues.

Often associated with flare during acceleration from 25-45 mph.
safetyImpact: Harsh or slipping shift, possible limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Fluid service
fixSteps.instruction: ATF and filter.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Replace 2-3 shift solenoid.

faq.q: Why does 2-3 slip most under load?
faq.a: Slipping clutches rely on hydraulic pressure; under load pressure requirements are higher, so wear shows first during acceleration.

title: 3-4 Shift Malfunction
shortDescription: The 3-4 upshift is not completing correctly.
fullDescription:
P0783 indicates the 3-4 shift is defective — slipping, not occurring, or harsh. On 4-speed automatics 3-4 is the overdrive engagement, common failure point.

Overdrive clutch wear and solenoid issues are the most common causes.
safetyImpact: High RPM on highway, reduced fuel economy, possible slipping.

fixSteps.title: Fluid service
fixSteps.instruction: Fresh ATF and filter.

fixSteps.title: Solenoid or rebuild
fixSteps.instruction: Replace solenoid or rebuild transmission.

faq.a: Yes but fuel economy will suffer and engine wear accelerates at highway speeds.

title: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: Intermediate shaft speed sensor circuit fault.
fullDescription:
P0791 sets when the TCM detects a fault in the intermediate shaft speed sensor circuit. Some transmissions use an intermediate shaft sensor for precise shift timing.

Causes include a failed sensor, damaged tone ring, or wiring issues.
safetyImpact: Shift timing off, possible limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Inspect sensor and wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Remove sensor, check tip and connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Install new sensor.

faq.a: If the sensor is externally accessible, no — it is a common replacement.

title: Pressure Control Solenoid C Malfunction
shortDescription: Fault detected in pressure control solenoid C circuit.
fullDescription:
P0795 sets when the TCM detects a malfunction in PCS-C. On transmissions with three or more pressure solenoids, PCS-C controls a specific clutch-pack pressure.

Diagnosis follows the same path as other pressure solenoids — electrical checks, then fluid service, then valve body.
safetyImpact: Shift problems in gears controlled by PCS-C.

fixSteps.title: Electrical test
fixSteps.instruction: Measure solenoid resistance.

fixSteps.title: Replace solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Replace PCS-C in valve body.

faq.a: Depends on the transmission. Consult the service manual for the specific unit.

title: Pressure Control Solenoid C Electrical
shortDescription: Electrical fault in PCS-C circuit.
fullDescription:
P0798 is the electrical subfault for PCS-C. The TCM detects an open, short, or resistance out of range on the PCS-C control circuit.

Check wiring and connector before assuming internal failure.
safetyImpact: Harsh shifts or limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Resistance test
fixSteps.instruction: Measure at external connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace
fixSteps.instruction: Replace solenoid or harness.

faq.q: How can I test without dropping the pan?
faq.a: The external transmission connector allows testing from outside — consult service manual for pin-outs.

title: Reverse Inhibit Control Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: Fault in the reverse inhibit control circuit.
fullDescription:
P0801 sets when the PCM/TCM detects a fault in the reverse inhibit circuit, which prevents shifting into reverse while the vehicle is moving forward.

Causes include a failed solenoid, wiring fault, or shift linkage switch issue.
safetyImpact: Reverse inhibit may not function (safety concern) or may not release in reverse.

fixSteps.title: Test solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance at connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace solenoid or repair wiring.

faq.a: It's the safety system that prevents you from shifting into reverse while moving forward at speed.

title: Clutch Position Control Error
shortDescription: Clutch position sensor control error.
fullDescription:
P0810 applies to vehicles with electronically controlled clutches (dual-clutch automatics and some manuals with assist). The TCM has detected that the commanded clutch position does not match the actual position feedback.

Causes include a failed clutch actuator, worn clutch requiring calibration, or position sensor failure.
safetyImpact: Shift quality problems, may become undrivable.

fixSteps.title: Clutch adaptation
fixSteps.instruction: Perform clutch relearn/adaptation with appropriate scan tool.

fixSteps.title: Replace clutch if worn
fixSteps.instruction: Replace clutch assembly if calibration fails.

faq.a: The TCM learns the wear point of the clutch; adaptation resets this reference after service or wear.

title: Upshift Switch Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: Upshift switch circuit fault.
fullDescription:
P0815 applies to vehicles with a manual-shift mode (paddle shifters or +/- shifter). The TCM detects a fault in the upshift request signal — the switch may be stuck, missing, or shorted.

Causes include a failed paddle/button, wiring damage, or clock-spring failure (for steering-wheel paddles).
safetyImpact: Manual-shift mode may not work. Automatic mode unaffected.

fixSteps.title: Test switch
fixSteps.instruction: Verify with scan tool live data when pressing paddle.

fixSteps.title: Replace switch/clock-spring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace faulty component.

faq.a: Not really — automatic mode handles normal driving, so this is a minor fault.

title: Up and Down Shift Switch Circuit
shortDescription: Fault in combined up/down shift switch circuit.
fullDescription:
P0826 sets when the PCM detects a fault in the shift +/- switch circuit. Similar to P0815/P0816 but for combined switches or when both are affected.

Commonly caused by a failed shift switch or harness wear.
safetyImpact: Manual-shift mode disabled.

fixSteps.title: Scan live data
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm switch signal missing/erratic.

fixSteps.title: Replace switch
fixSteps.instruction: Replace shift switch assembly.

faq.a: Usually no — emissions/drivability still work normally.

title: Four Wheel Drive Switch Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: Fault in the 4WD switch circuit.
fullDescription:
P0836 sets when the PCM cannot read a valid 4WD engagement switch signal. The switch tells the PCM if 4WD is requested.

Causes include a failed dash switch, wiring issue, or failed transfer case actuator position switch.
safetyImpact: 4WD may not engage when needed.

fixSteps.title: Check dash switch
fixSteps.instruction: Verify switch click and continuity.

fixSteps.title: Check transfer case switch
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect position switch at transfer case.

fixSteps.title: Replace as needed
fixSteps.instruction: Swap faulty switch.

faq.a: Not necessarily — P0836 is a switch fault, not a mechanical transfer case issue.

title: Transmission Fluid Pressure Sensor A Range/Performance
shortDescription: TFP sensor A reading is out of expected range.
fullDescription:
P0841 sets when the transmission fluid pressure sensor A (TFP-A) reports a value that doesn't match operating conditions — pressure should rise with load, gear engagement, etc., and if it doesn't, this code sets.

Causes include a failed sensor, low line pressure (pump issues), or wiring/connector problems.
safetyImpact: Harsh shifts or slipping due to incorrect pressure feedback.

fixSteps.title: Check ATF level
fixSteps.instruction: Low fluid will cause pressure issues.

fixSteps.title: Test sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Live data, compare to spec.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace TFP-A; internal access may be required.

faq.q: Can low fluid really cause this?
faq.a: Yes — insufficient fluid means the pump can't build full pressure.

title: Transmission Fluid Pressure Sensor B Circuit
shortDescription: Fault in TFP sensor B circuit.
fullDescription:
P0845 is set when the TCM detects an issue with the transmission fluid pressure sensor B circuit. Some transmissions use multiple pressure sensors for different circuits.

Diagnosis follows the same pattern as P0841 but for the B sensor.
safetyImpact: Shift quality problems.

fixSteps.title: Scan live data
fixSteps.instruction: Compare pressure readings to spec.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check external connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace TFP-B.

faq.a: No — pressure feedback is critical to safe transmission operation. Repair promptly.

title: Transmission Fluid Pressure Low
shortDescription: Overall transmission fluid pressure is below specification.
fullDescription:
P0868 sets when the TCM detects that overall line pressure is too low for the current operating conditions. This is a serious code that usually indicates a mechanical pump or seal problem.

Causes include worn pump, internal leakage, clogged filter, or low fluid.
safetyImpact: Severe slipping, risk of transmission destruction.

fixSteps.title: Check ATF level
fixSteps.instruction: If low, top off and check for external leaks.

fixSteps.title: Service fluid and filter
fixSteps.instruction: Replace filter and fluid.

fixSteps.title: Pressure test
fixSteps.instruction: Connect mechanical pressure gauge to test ports.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild/replace
fixSteps.instruction: Internal pump/seal replacement.

faq.a: No — low pressure causes rapid wear and possible complete transmission failure. Tow it.

title: Transmission Fluid Pressure Sensor C Circuit
shortDescription: Fault in TFP sensor C circuit.
fullDescription:
P0871 indicates a circuit fault in TFP-C. Similar diagnosis to P0841/P0845 but for the C pressure sensor.

Check wiring first, then test sensor.
safetyImpact: Shift quality issues.

fixSteps.title: Inspect connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check transmission external connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace TFP-C.

faq.a: Modern transmissions monitor multiple pressure circuits to control clutches independently.

title: TCM Power Input Signal Low
shortDescription: TCM power supply voltage is too low.
fullDescription:
P0882 is set when the TCM sees low supply voltage. The TCM needs stable power to operate — low voltage can cause erratic shifting and limp mode.

Causes include weak battery, failing alternator, corroded grounds, or bad fuse/relay supplying TCM.
safetyImpact: Erratic shifting, limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Test battery and charging
fixSteps.instruction: Battery should hold charge, alternator 13.8-14.7V running.

fixSteps.title: Check TCM power/ground
fixSteps.instruction: Measure voltage at TCM connector, check ground continuity.

fixSteps.title: Replace battery/alternator
fixSteps.instruction: Replace faulty charging component.

faq.q: Could a bad battery cause transmission codes?
faq.a: Yes — modern transmissions require stable voltage, and weak batteries trigger many electronic faults.

title: Transmission Component Slipping
shortDescription: Internal transmission component is slipping.
fullDescription:
P0894 is set when the TCM detects an unspecified internal slippage — input/output ratios don't match for a given commanded gear, but the TCM cannot identify which specific gear.

Usually indicates progressive internal wear or low pressure across multiple circuits.
safetyImpact: Power loss, possible transmission failure.

fixSteps.title: Fluid inspection and service
fixSteps.instruction: Check condition, replace if burnt/dirty.

fixSteps.title: Pressure test
fixSteps.instruction: Mechanical pressure gauge at test ports.

fixSteps.title: Professional diagnosis
fixSteps.instruction: Likely rebuild required.

faq.q: Is my transmission going to fail soon?
faq.a: P0894 usually means significant wear. Professional inspection is urgent to prevent complete failure.

title: Clutch Actuator Circuit/Open
shortDescription: Clutch actuator circuit is open.
fullDescription:
P0900 sets on vehicles with automated clutch systems (DCT, automated manual, eClutch). The TCM detects the clutch actuator circuit is open — no current flow to the motor or solenoid.

Causes include failed actuator, broken wiring, or TCM driver failure.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not move or may become immobile.

fixSteps.title: Inspect actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Visual and electrical inspection of actuator assembly.

fixSteps.title: Replace actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Replace failed clutch actuator and perform calibration.

faq.a: DCT transmissions (VW DSG, Ford PowerShift, Porsche PDK) and some hybrid eClutch systems.

title: Gear Shift Position Circuit
shortDescription: Gear shift position circuit fault.
fullDescription:
P0914 indicates the TCM cannot reliably determine which gear is engaged based on gear-position feedback. Applies to both automated manuals and dual-clutch transmissions.

Causes include failed gear position sensor, wiring fault, or mechanical gear fork issue.
safetyImpact: Poor shifts, wrong gear engagement, or no movement.

fixSteps.title: Scan live data
fixSteps.instruction: Review gear position sensor feedback vs commanded.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor/harness
fixSteps.instruction: Replace faulty component and recalibrate.

faq.a: Yes — DCTs rely heavily on accurate gear position feedback, and these codes are fairly common as they age.

title: Gear Shift Position Control Error
shortDescription: The commanded gear shift position is not being achieved.
fullDescription:
P0919 is set when the TCM commands a specific gear position (via shift actuator) but the feedback does not confirm the position was reached within the allowable time.

Typically means a mechanical issue — sticky shift fork, worn synchros, or failing shift actuator.
safetyImpact: Transmission stuck between gears possible. Limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Calibration/adaptation
fixSteps.instruction: Perform shift actuator calibration with OEM tool.

fixSteps.title: Replace actuator
fixSteps.instruction: If calibration fails, replace shift actuator.

fixSteps.title: Transmission rebuild
fixSteps.instruction: Mechanical wear requires internal service.

faq.a: Very risky — transmission may get stuck and leave you stranded. Tow to a specialist.
